mappings.rationaleAI: The Vedas are the primary texts of the Vedic period (A.3) — covering the Rigveda (A.3.a) and later Vedas and Brahmanas (A.3.b) including the Upanishads (A.3.c) — and constitute the revealed scriptures of B.1.a and the foundational post-Vedic Sanskrit literature of B.1.b.

Jainism is the direct subject of D.1, covering its history through the Tirthankaras and Mahavira, Jaina mythology, and the beliefs, practices, and institutions of the tradition including ahimsa, anekantavada, and ascetic orders.

Adi Shankara systematized Advaita Vedanta — one of the most influential schools of Hindu philosophy (B.3) and mysticism (B.4) — and his 8th-century reforms correspond to A.7 (rise of devotional Hinduism), while Advaita addresses C.2.a (Vedism and Brahmanism) and views about God/Brahman.

Buddhism emerged in the heterodox period (A.4) as one of the primary challenges to Brahmanism by reformers and ascetic groups, making it essential context for understanding Hinduism's development during the 7th–2nd century BCE.
Sikhism is the direct subject of D.2, covering its founding by Guru Nanak, the ten Gurus, the Guru Granth Sahib, Khalsa formation, and Sikh beliefs, practices, and institutions.

The Bhagavad Gita is the most influential post-Vedic Sanskrit text (B.1.b), synthesizing Hindu mythology (B.3), the paths of dharma, karma, and bhakti (C.1.b, C.1.c), and providing a core scriptural basis for Vaishnavism (C.2.b).

The Indus Valley Civilisation is explicitly named at A.2 (prehistoric and protohistoric periods, through the 2nd millennium BCE) as one of the two primary subjects — alongside the religions of indigenous prehistoric peoples — making this a direct and priority fit.

The Gupta Empire (mid-3rd to mid-6th century CE) corresponds to both the early Hindu period when major sects rose (A.5) and the Puranic period of Hindu consolidation (A.6), and is notable for Mahabharata/Ramayana canonization and Sanskrit literary patronage.

Gandhi exemplifies the modern period of Hinduism (A.9) and specifically the ethical and nationalist movements in modern Hinduism (C.2.f) through his application of ahimsa and Hindu ethics to anti-colonial politics.

Akbar's reign spans the age of bhakti (A.8) and his Din-i Ilahi syncretic movement represents a key instance of Hindu-Islamic interaction; his abolition of the jizya and Hindu-Muslim conciliation provide context for Sikhism's growth (D.2.a.vi).

The Mughal Empire spans the age of bhakti (A.8) through the modern period (A.9), providing the political and cultural context for Hinduism's development including bhakti movements and Hindu-Islamic interaction, and Sikhism's establishment and militarism under Mughal rule (D.2).

The Buddha's life and heterodox teachings were a primary challenge to Brahmanism in the heterodox period (A.4), and he was a contemporary of Mahavira (D.1.a.ii), making his article essential context for both Hinduism's response and Jainism's background.

The Vedas were preserved through elaborate oral mnemonic transmission for millennia before writing, making oral tradition directly relevant to B.1.a (primary Vedic scriptures as eternal revelations) and B.1.c (sacred literature in Indian regional languages transmitted orally).

Covers the typology of divine beings relevant to Hindu mythology (B.2), views about God or the sacred in Hindu belief (C.1.a), and the deity-centered traditions of Vaishnavism (C.2.b) and Shaivism (C.2.c).

The Achaemenid Empire provides the historical background for Zoroastrianism that preceded the Parsi community's migration to India; the Parsis (D.3) are Zoroastrians who fled Persian Islamic conquest and whose Iranian religious heritage begins with the Achaemenid era.

Covers Indian religious art including symbols, iconography, temple sculpture, and the theological function of icons in expressing Hinduism — directly relevant to C.4 (cultural expressions of Hindu values) and C.4.a (traditional religious functions of Indian art, yantras, mandalas, icons).

Covers Indian philosophy including the six orthodox Hindu schools (Nyaya, Vaisheshika, Samkhya, Yoga, Mimamsa, Vedanta), directly relevant to B.3 (Hindu philosophy: the integral relation of philosophy and religion in Hinduism).

Provides broad cross-cultural framing for understanding Hinduism within the global context of religious traditions, covering Indian religions including Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, and Sikhism (A, C, D) at an overview level.

The British Empire's rule of India shaped the modern period of Hinduism (A.9) including reform movements and nationalist Hinduism, and directly constitutes the context of D.2.a.v (Sikhism under British rule, 19th–20th century).

Colonial rule reshaped modern Hinduism (A.9) and the Sikh community's identity and institutions under British rule (D.2.a.v) and in independent India and Pakistan (D.2.a.vi), with colonial modernity driving Hindu reform and nationalist movements.

Alexander's campaigns into northwestern India and the subsequent Hellenistic cultural exchange contributed to the context of the early Hindu period (A.5), including the rise of Greco-Buddhist artistic conventions that influenced Indian religious iconography.
